CLINICAL INDICATION: 23-year-old male with sudden onset left-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea. No history of trauma. Evaluate for pneumothorax.

TECHNIQUE: PA and lateral views of the chest obtained in the upright position with good inspiratory effort. Good technical quality.

FINDINGS:
Lungs: There is a large left-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the left lung. A visceral pleural line is clearly visualized with an estimated apical interpleural distance of greater than 3 cm. No contralateral mediastinal shift is identified at this time, though the mediastinum appears mildly displaced toward the right. No pleural effusion is identified on the left. The right lung is clear without focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax.

Pleura: As described above. No pleural effusion bilaterally.

Mediastinum: Mild rightward mediastinal shift noted. Cardiac silhouette is mildly displaced to the right. No evidence of tension physiology at this time.

Heart: Cardiac size is difficult to assess given mediastinal shift; however, no gross cardiomegaly is appreciated.

Bones and soft tissues: Ribs, clavicles, and visible osseous structures are intact without fracture. No subcutaneous emphysema identified. Soft tissues are otherwise unremarkable.

Tubes and lines: None present.

IMPRESSION:
1. Large left-sided pneumothorax with near-complete collapse of the left lung and mild rightward mediastinal displacement. Clinical correlation is recommended to exclude early tension physiology; urgent clinical evaluation is advised.
2. No contralateral pneumothorax. No pleural effusion.
3. Findings are most consistent with a spontaneous pneumothorax in this young male patient; underlying pulmonary blebs/bullae are a clinical consideration and may be further evaluated following treatment.
